verses.text: In the second year of Joash son of Jehoahaz king of Israel reigned Amaziah the son of Joash king of Judah. He was twenty and five years old when he began to reign, and reigned twenty and nine years in Jerusalem. And his mother's name was Jehoaddan of Jerusalem. And he did that which was right in the sight of the LORD, yet not like David his father: he did according to all things as Joash his father did. Howbeit the high places were not taken away: as yet the people did sacrifice and burnt incense on the high places. Guidance: Amaziah introduced — right, but graded against David. Amaziah son of the murdered Joash begins to reign in Judah at 25, reigning 29 years. His mother was Jehoaddan of Jerusalem. The verdict carries a careful comparison: 'he did that which was right in the sight of the LORD, yet not like David his father: he did according to all things as Joash his father did.' The narrator measures Judah's kings against two standards — David (the gold standard of wholehearted devotion) and the immediate predecessor. Amaziah passes the lower bar (like Joash, basically right) but fails the higher one (not like David). And the now-standard Judah flaw recurs: 'the high places were not taken away.' The chapter is showing us a king of the middle grade — better than the apostate northerners, worse than the wholehearted David. The chapter is teaching us that there are degrees of faithfulness, and that being 'better than the bad kings' is not the same as being like David. Amaziah inherits both his father Joash's basic orthodoxy AND his father's compromises (the high places) — and, as the chapter will show, also his father's later instability of heart (2 Chr 25 records that Amaziah turned to Edomite idols after his Edom victory, which explains the disasters that follow). The chapter is teaching us that a faith modeled on a flawed parent tends to reproduce both the parent's virtues and the parent's defects, unless the child reaches past the parent to David's God directly.

And it came to pass, as soon as the kingdom was confirmed in his hand, that he slew his servants which had slain the king his father. But the children of the murderers he slew not: according unto that which is written in the book of the law of Moses, wherein the LORD commanded, saying, The fathers shall not be put to death for the children, nor the children be put to death for the fathers; but every man shall be put to death for his own sin. Guidance: Justice bounded by the law of Moses. Once securely on the throne, Amaziah executes the servants who had assassinated his father Joash (12:20-21). But he stops there: 'the children of the murderers he slew not.' The narrator highlights why — Amaziah obeyed the written law: 'according unto that which is written in the book of the law of Moses... The fathers shall not be put to death for the children, nor the children be put to death for the fathers; but every man shall be put to death for his own sin' (Deut 24:16). The chapter is showing us a rare and commendable moment of a king submitting his power to Scripture. The customary ancient-Near-East practice was to wipe out the whole family of an assassin to prevent future vengeance; Amaziah resists that political instinct and follows the Mosaic principle of individual responsibility — each person bears guilt for his own sin, not his father's. The chapter is teaching us a foundational principle of biblical justice: guilt is personal, not hereditary. The law restrains the natural human impulse toward dynastic revenge. The chapter is also showing us what it looks like when a ruler lets the written word of God govern his exercise of power — Amaziah had every motive and cultural license to kill the children, and he refrained because the law forbade it. The chapter is teaching us that submission to Scripture often means NOT doing what power makes possible and culture makes normal. This is Amaziah at his best — a king who reads the law and obeys it even against his own advantage. It makes his later folly (rashly provoking Israel) all the sadder by contrast.

He slew of Edom in the valley of salt ten thousand, and took Selah by war, and called the name of it Joktheel unto this day. Guidance: The Edom victory. Amaziah campaigns successfully against Edom, killing 10,000 in the Valley of Salt (the arid basin south of the Dead Sea) and capturing Selah ('the rock' — likely the great rock-fortress later known as Petra), which he renames Joktheel. The chapter records a genuine military success that recovered some of Judah's lost dominance over Edom (which had revolted under Jehoram, 8:20-22). The chapter is showing us the high point of Amaziah's reign — a real victory, the kind that should have produced humble gratitude to the LORD who gave it. But the chapter is also planting the seed of his downfall. 2 Chronicles 25 fills in what Kings omits: after this victory Amaziah brought back the gods of Edom and worshipped them — bowing to the very gods of the people he had just defeated, a folly the prophet rebuked by asking why he sought gods that could not deliver their own people. And the victory inflated his pride, leading directly to the rash challenge to Israel that follows. The chapter is teaching us the spiritual danger of success: a victory received without humility becomes the occasion for both idolatry and arrogance. The same triumph that should have deepened Amaziah's trust in the LORD instead puffed up his heart. The chapter is preparing us to watch a man undone not by defeat but by what he did with his victory.

Then Amaziah sent messengers to Jehoash, the son of Jehoahaz son of Jehu, king of Israel, saying, Come, let us look one another in the face. And Jehoash the king of Israel sent to Amaziah king of Judah, saying, The thistle that was in Lebanon sent to the cedar that was in Lebanon, saying, Give thy daughter to my son to wife: and there passed by a wild beast that was in Lebanon, and trode down the thistle. Thou hast indeed smitten Edom, and thine heart hath lifted thee up: glory of this, and tarry at home: for why shouldest thou meddle to thy hurt, that thou shouldest fall, even thou, and Judah with thee? Guidance: The rash challenge and Jehoash's fable. Inflated by his Edom victory, Amaziah picks a fight with the much stronger northern kingdom: 'Come, let us look one another in the face' — a challenge to battle. Jehoash of Israel replies with a cutting fable. A lowly thistle in Lebanon presumptuously demanded that the mighty cedar give its daughter in marriage to the thistle's son — and a passing wild beast simply trampled the thistle flat. The meaning is unmistakable: Amaziah (the thistle) is absurdly overreaching against Israel (the cedar), and will be crushed as easily as a beast tramples a weed. Jehoash then drops the metaphor and gives plain, almost merciful counsel: 'Thou hast indeed smitten Edom, and thine heart hath lifted thee up: glory of this, and tarry at home: for why shouldest thou meddle to thy hurt, that thou shouldest fall, even thou, and Judah with thee?' He diagnoses the real problem — pride from the Edom victory — and urges Amaziah to enjoy his glory and stay home. The chapter is showing us the precise mechanism of a proud man's ruin: a real success ('thou hast indeed smitten Edom') becomes inflated self-regard ('thine heart hath lifted thee up'), which drives a reckless overreach. The chapter is teaching us that pride born of one victory will gamble away everything by seeking a fight it cannot win. Jehoash's warning is shrewd and even generous; he would rather Amaziah back down than be destroyed. The chapter is teaching us to receive the warnings that pride makes us deaf to — the wisest counsel often comes from a rival who has nothing to gain by lying, and Jehoash here speaks plain truth Amaziah desperately needs to hear.

But Amaziah would not hear. Therefore Jehoash king of Israel went up; and he and Amaziah king of Judah looked one another in the face at Bethshemesh, which belongeth to Judah. And Judah was put to the worse before Israel; and they fled every man to their tents. And Jehoash king of Israel took Amaziah king of Judah, the son of Jehoash the son of Ahaziah, at Bethshemesh, and came to Jerusalem, and brake down the wall of Jerusalem from the gate of Ephraim unto the corner gate, four hundred cubits. And he took all the gold and silver, and all the vessels that were found in the house of the LORD, and in the treasures of the king's house, and hostages, and returned to Samaria. Guidance: The defeat at Beth-shemesh and the humbling of Jerusalem. 'Amaziah would not hear' — the four words that seal his ruin. He rejects the warning, and the war goes exactly as Jehoash predicted. The armies meet at Beth-shemesh in Judah's own territory; Judah is routed; the soldiers flee. Jehoash captures Amaziah himself, marches to Jerusalem, and breaks down 400 cubits (about 600 feet) of the city wall from the Ephraim gate to the corner gate — leaving the capital defenseless. He plunders all the gold, silver, and vessels of both the temple and the palace, takes hostages, and returns to Samaria in triumph. The chapter is showing us the full harvest of unheeded pride. Amaziah's refusal to listen cost Judah a military defeat, a captured king, a breached capital, an emptied temple, and hostages carried off. The very temple treasures his father Joash had given to buy off Hazael (12:18) — and whatever had been replenished — are now looted again. The chapter is teaching us that 'would not hear' is among the most expensive phrases in Scripture: the warning was free, clear, and correct, and the cost of ignoring it was catastrophic. The chapter is also showing us the LORD's hand in the defeat — 2 Chr 25:20 says 'it came of God, that he might deliver them into the hand of their enemies, because they sought after the gods of Edom.' The military disaster was the outworking of Amaziah's idolatry; his proud overreach was the means by which his idolatry met its judgment. The chapter is teaching us that the consequences of a hardened heart are not merely natural but providential — God lets the proud man's folly become the instrument of his correction.

Now the rest of the acts of Jehoash which he did, and his might, and how he fought with Amaziah king of Judah, are they not written in the book of the chronicles of the kings of Israel? And Jehoash slept with his fathers, and was buried in Samaria with the kings of Israel; and Jeroboam his son reigned in his stead. Guidance: Jehoash of Israel's close — a repeated formula. The narrator gives Jehoash of Israel his regnal-closing notice a second time (he already summarized it in 13:12-13), now in its proper chronological slot after the Beth-shemesh war. He died, was buried in Samaria with the kings of Israel, and his son Jeroboam (Jeroboam II) succeeded. The chapter is showing us the orderly succession of the Jehu dynasty continuing: Jehu → Jehoahaz → Jehoash → Jeroboam II — three of the promised four generations now seated. The chapter is teaching us, through the bookkeeping, that the LORD's word to Jehu (10:30) is steadily being fulfilled generation by generation, even as these kings remain under the calf-worship indictment. The double-mention of Jehoash's death (here and 13:13) reflects the narrator's method of grouping material thematically (the Elisha death-scene clustered in ch 13) while also keeping the king-by-king chronology; it is not a contradiction but the seams of how the book is assembled. The chapter is teaching us, in passing, that Scripture's history is arranged to make theological points (Elisha's last prophecy, the Jehu promise) as much as to record a bare timeline.

And Amaziah the son of Joash king of Judah lived after the death of Jehoash son of Jehoahaz king of Israel fifteen years. And the rest of the acts of Amaziah, are they not written in the book of the chronicles of the kings of Judah? Now they made a conspiracy against him in Jerusalem: and he fled to Lachish; but they sent after him to Lachish, and slew him there. And they brought him on horses: and he was buried at Jerusalem with his fathers in the city of David. Guidance: Amaziah's assassination. Amaziah outlives his conqueror Jehoash by 15 years, but his end mirrors his father's. A conspiracy arises against him in Jerusalem; he flees to Lachish (a fortified city in the lowlands); the conspirators pursue him there and kill him. His body is brought back on horses and buried with his fathers in the city of David. The chapter is showing us another Davidic king dying by assassination — the second in a row (Joash, then Amaziah). (2 Chr 25:27 dates the rise of the conspiracy 'from the time that Amaziah did turn away from following the LORD,' tying his death to his post-victory idolatry.) The chapter is teaching us the long, grinding cost of a king's turn from the LORD: the Edom idols he embraced led to the proud war with Israel, the defeat, the breached capital — and ultimately a fatal loss of his people's loyalty and his own life. The downward slope is consistent: a man who began by obeying the law of Moses (v6) and won a real victory (v7) ends fleeing his own capital and murdered in exile. The chapter is teaching us that the trajectory of a life can reverse — early obedience does not guarantee a faithful end, and a heart lifted up by success can carry a man from law-keeping to idolatry to ruin. The pattern of assassinated Davidic kings also signals Judah's growing instability, foreshadowing the deeper troubles to come.

And all the people of Judah took Azariah, which was sixteen years old, and made him king instead of his father Amaziah. He built Elath, and restored it to Judah, after that the king slept with his fathers. Guidance: Azariah (Uzziah) raised up. After Amaziah's murder, the people of Judah make his 16-year-old son Azariah (also called Uzziah) king. His first noted act: he 'built Elath, and restored it to Judah' — Elath being the port on the Gulf of Aqaba, key to Red Sea trade, recovered for Judah. The chapter is showing us the resilience of the Davidic line: despite assassinations and disasters, 'all the people of Judah' rally to set David's heir on the throne. The covenant lamp keeps burning. The chapter is also introducing one of Judah's most significant and longest-reigning kings (52 years, ch 15): Uzziah, under whom Judah would reach great prosperity and military strength (detailed in 2 Chr 26), and during whose reign Isaiah, Amos, and Hosea would begin to prophesy. The recovery of Elath signals a turn toward renewed Judean strength after Amaziah's debacle. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD's purposes for His people continue across the failures of individual kings — a murdered father is succeeded by a son who rebuilds and restores. The young king's reign begins on a constructive note (building, restoring) that contrasts with his father's destructive pride. The chapter is preparing us for the fuller account of Uzziah in the next chapter, where his greatness and his tragic later pride (the leprosy) will both be told.

In the fifteenth year of Amaziah the son of Joash king of Judah Jeroboam the son of Joash king of Israel began to reign in Samaria, and reigned forty and one years. And he did that which was evil in the sight of the LORD: he departed not from all the sins of Jeroboam the son of Nebat, who made Israel to sin. He restored the coast of Israel from the entering of Hamath unto the sea of the plain, according to the word of the LORD God of Israel, which he spake by the hand of his servant Jonah, the son of Amittai, the prophet, which was of Gathhepher. Guidance: Jeroboam II — great success under an unchanged verdict. Jeroboam II reigns 41 years — the longest northern reign, a period of remarkable prosperity and territorial expansion. Yet the verdict is unchanged: 'he did that which was evil in the sight of the LORD: he departed not from all the sins of Jeroboam the son of Nebat.' He restored Israel's borders 'from the entering of Hamath unto the sea of the plain' (the Dead Sea) — recovering nearly the full extent of the kingdom's ideal boundaries. And the narrator notes this was 'according to the word of the LORD... which he spake by the hand of his servant Jonah, the son of Amittai, the prophet, which was of Gath-hepher.' (This is the same Jonah of the book of Jonah — here shown as a prophet who also delivered a word of national restoration for Israel.) The chapter is showing us a striking truth: the LORD granted Israel its greatest prosperity in centuries through a king He still calls evil. The expansion was not a reward for righteousness — Jeroboam kept the calves — but a sovereign gift fulfilling a prophetic word. The chapter is teaching us that material success and territorial greatness are not proof of God's approval. Jeroboam II's golden age was real, but it coexisted with deep spiritual rot (the era Amos and Hosea would denounce for its injustice, luxury, and idolatry). The chapter is teaching us to read prosperity carefully: God may grant abundance even to the disobedient for His own purposes, and outward success can mask the inner decay that prophets see and that judgment will eventually expose.

For the LORD saw the affliction of Israel, that it was very bitter: for there was not any shut up, nor any left, nor any helper for Israel. And the LORD said not that he would blot out the name of Israel from under heaven: but he saved them by the hand of Jeroboam the son of Joash. Guidance: Why the LORD granted the deliverance: His pity, not their merit. The narrator explains the theology behind Jeroboam II's victories. 'The LORD saw the affliction of Israel, that it was very bitter: for there was not any shut up, nor any left, nor any helper for Israel.' Israel had been ground down (by Aram, ch 13) to a desperate, helpless state — no one to deliver them. 'And the LORD said not that he would blot out the name of Israel from under heaven: but he saved them by the hand of Jeroboam.' The chapter is showing us, once again, the same pattern as 13:4-5 and 13:23: the LORD's deliverance of Israel flows from His compassion for their suffering and His covenant faithfulness, NOT from any repentance or worthiness on their part. He saw their bitter affliction and, moved by pity, granted relief through an evil king. The phrase 'said not that he would blot out the name of Israel from under heaven' is poignant — the time for that final blotting-out had not yet come (it will come in ch 17), so the LORD extends mercy and restoration even now. The chapter is teaching us the astonishing patience of God: He keeps rescuing a people who keep sinning, because He sees their suffering and remembers His covenant. The chapter is also teaching us that divine mercy granted to the undeserving is not approval of their sin — the same God who saves them through Jeroboam will, when the appointed time comes, hand them to Assyria. The deliverance is a stay of execution, an extension of grace meant to give room for repentance — repentance Israel will tragically refuse.

Now the rest of the acts of Jeroboam, and all that he did, and his might, how he warred, and how he recovered Damascus, and Hamath, which belonged to Judah, for Israel, are they not written in the book of the chronicles of the kings of Israel? And Jeroboam slept with his fathers, even with the kings of Israel; and Zachariah his son reigned in his stead. Guidance: Jeroboam II's close — and the fourth generation arrives. The narrator notes Jeroboam's military greatness — his recovery of Damascus and Hamath (the great Aramean centers, now brought under Israelite control). He dies, buried with the kings of Israel, and his son Zachariah (Zechariah) succeeds. The chapter is closing the reign of the north's most powerful king since Solomon's era — yet it is closed with the same understated formula as the worst kings, because greatness in war never altered the spiritual verdict. The crucial detail is the successor: 'Zachariah his son reigned in his stead.' Zechariah is the FOURTH generation of Jehu's line (Jehu → Jehoahaz → Jehoash → Jeroboam II → Zechariah). The LORD's promise to Jehu (10:30) — 'thy children of the fourth generation shall sit on the throne of Israel' — is about to reach both its fulfillment and its limit. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD's word sets boundaries as precisely as it grants blessings: four generations, no more. Jeroboam II's long, prosperous reign was the high-water mark of the dynasty; with Zechariah, the promised line will end (next chapter, after a mere six months). The chapter is showing us a kingdom at the peak of its power and the edge of its collapse simultaneously — the borders are restored, Damascus is subdued, and yet the dynastic clock the LORD set is about to strike its final hour. The greatest external strength conceals the nearest internal end.
